sound_type: waka-waka-waka -- raucous laughing call, rapid nasal series, colonial vocalization, social and frequent
ecosystem: Oak savanna -- open oak woodland, park-like landscapes dominated by oaks. Southern Oregon at northern range edge in PNW.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Colonial. Cooperative breeder.

coordinates.description: Reference for oak savanna habitat in southern Oregon. Acorn Woodpeckers found in oak woodlands at the northern edge of their range in the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: PICIDAE RETURNS after a 53-degree gap. Melanerpes is a new genus in the engine. Previously: Colaptes (Northern Flicker, 21-22), Sphyrapicus (Red-breasted Sapsucker, 54), Dryocopus (Pileated Woodpecker, 54). Four Picidae species total.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 4 (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Melanerpes from Greek melas ('black') + herpes ('creeper'). formicivorus from Latin formica ('ant') + vorare ('to devour') -- 'ant-eater,' though the species is better known for acorn storage. 'Acorn Woodpecker' is descriptive of the species' signature behavior.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Melanerpes is new to the engine at degree 107. A large New World genus of woodpeckers. Includes the Red-headed Woodpecker and Lewis's Woodpecker. The Acorn Woodpecker is the most colonial member of the genus.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Returns to the engine after 53 degrees. The Corvidae septet (100-106) was Passeriformes; the Acorn Woodpecker shifts the engine to Piciformes.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae returns after the longest gap since its last appearance (54 to 107 = 53 degrees). After the Corvidae septet, the engine shifts to a new family.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,000-4,000 Hz. Raucous, nasal, laughing waka-waka-waka calls. Broadband with harsh spectral characteristics.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,500-3,000 Hz. The raucous quality comes from rapid repetition of nasal notes with harsh overtones.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary waka-waka-waka: 1,500-3,500 Hz, nasal, rapid, laughing. Contact calls: shorter waka or ka notes. Alarm: harsher, more emphatic versions.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual waka: 0.1-0.2 seconds. Series: 1-4 seconds of rapid waka-waka-waka. Colonial calling sessions: can persist for minutes with multiple birds.
song_metrics.note_structure: RAUCOUS WAKA: Rapid series of harsh, nasal, laughing notes -- waka waka waka waka. Clownish quality. Diagnostic among PNW woodpeckers. Faster and more nasal than the Pileated Woodpecker's wild laughing call.
song_metrics.note_types: Waka-waka-waka (primary, social/contact), shorter ka notes (contact), alarm calls (harsher), drumming (territorial, on granary trees and resonant wood). The vocal repertoire is social and frequent.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Rapid series. The waka-waka-waka is delivered in bursts. Colonial groups produce overlapping vocal textures. No fixed phrase structure -- social and conversational.
song_metrics.timbre: Nasal. Raucous. Laughing. Clownish. The most rapid-fire and comedic timbre among PNW woodpeckers. Distinct from the Pileated's wild, echoing laugh and the Flicker's sharp wicka-wicka-wicka.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- high energy. Loud, gregarious, persistent. Colonial groups calling simultaneously fill the oak woodland with raucous waka-waka-waka.
song_metrics.tempo: Fast. The waka-waka-waka is delivered at a rapid pace. Faster than the Pileated Woodpecker's laughing call.
song_metrics.context: SOCIAL AND COLONIAL. Contact calls between colony members, alarm calls, territorial vocalizations, and drumming. The Acorn Woodpecker vocalizes frequently throughout the day. Colonial communication is constant.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some variation across range. The diagnostic waka-waka-waka quality is consistent.
song_metrics.key_property: WAKA-WAKA-WAKA -- the colonial woodpecker. E=5. Raucous, nasal, laughing call. Granary trees with thousands of acorn-storage holes. Colonial living with cooperative breeding. Oak savanna habitat. PICIDAE RETURNS after 53-degree gap. Forty-seventh consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Bold facial pattern: white face with black crown and nape, red crown patch. Black back and wings with white rump and wing patches. White breast with black streaking. Black tail.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 107: (1) CLOWN FACE -- white face and forehead bordered by black, with red crown patch. Unmistakable. (2) BLACK AND WHITE -- bold pattern with white rump and wing patches visible in undulating flight. (3) RED CROWN -- bright red crown patch on black cap. (4) OAK HABITAT -- if you see a clown-faced woodpecker in oak woodland, it is an Acorn Woodpecker.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Males have red crown meeting white forehead; females have black band separating red crown from white forehead.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating flight typical of woodpeckers. White rump and wing patches flash conspicuously. Often seen flying between oak trees in colonial groups.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Body length 20-23 cm. Wingspan 35-43 cm. Weight 65-90 g. Bold black-and-white plumage with distinctive 'clown face' pattern -- white face, black crown border, red crown patch.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 107: clown-faced woodpecker in oak woodland. White face with black border and red crown. Colonial groups. Granary trees with thousands of holes. Waka-waka-waka call.
physical_description.field_id: Medium woodpecker with white face and 'clown' pattern in oak woodland. Raucous waka-waka-waka call. Usually in groups. Look for granary trees -- wooden surfaces riddled with individually drilled acorn-storage holes.

nodes.title: Waka-Waka-Waka -- The Colonial Woodpecker
nodes.description: Loud, raucous, rapid laughing call. E=5. Social and frequent. Colonial groups create a cacophony across oak savanna. Diagnostic -- no other PNW woodpecker sounds like this. Faster and more nasal than the Pileated's laughing call.

nodes.title: Picidae Returns -- First Woodpecker Since Degree 54
nodes.description: 53-degree gap since the last woodpecker. After the Corvidae septet (100-106), the engine shifts to Picidae. Fourth Picidae species: Flicker (21-22), Sapsucker (54), Pileated (54), Acorn (107). New genus Melanerpes.

nodes.title: Granary Trees -- Thousands of Drilled Holes
nodes.description: Community infrastructure. Thousands of custom-drilled holes, each storing one acorn. 50,000+ acorns per granary. Maintained across generations by the colonial group. Architecture at scale. Reused and expanded over decades.

nodes.title: Colonial Living -- Cooperative Breeding
nodes.description: Groups of 15+ individuals. Cooperative breeding, communal nest defense, shared granary maintenance. One of the most social woodpeckers in the world. Rare among Picidae. The colony as superorganism.

nodes.title: Oak Savanna -- Acorn Dependent
nodes.description: Open oak woodland and savanna. Southern Oregon at northern range edge in PNW. Without oaks, no acorns; without acorns, no Acorn Woodpecker. Habitat-obligate relationship.

cross_references.relationship: PICIDAE RETURNS after 53-degree gap. Pileated in old-growth forest (E=5 wild laughing); Acorn in oak savanna (E=5 waka-waka-waka). Both E=5 but different habitats and calls. Pileated is solitary; Acorn is colonial.

PICIDAE FAMILY. Engine's first woodpecker (degrees 21-22). Different feeding: Flicker feeds on ground (ants); Acorn stores in trees (acorns). Different social structure: Flicker largely solitary; Acorn colonial.

INTELLIGENCE PARALLEL. Both highly intelligent and social. Corvid intelligence (100) meets woodpecker social complexity (107). The Acorn Woodpecker's colonial system demonstrates high social intelligence.
COLONIAL PARALLEL. Marmot lives in colonies with shared burrow systems. Acorn Woodpecker lives in colonies with shared granary trees. Two colonial species, same community-infrastructure strategy.
FOOD CACHING PARALLEL. Nutcracker caches pine seeds (dispersed, spatial memory). Acorn Woodpecker caches acorns (concentrated, granary trees). Two caching strategies: dispersed vs concentrated.
